title: First Seoul
subtitle: Gwanghwamun / Jongno
blurb: The main palace, hanok streets, and the old-center stream in one day.
mood: History, courtyards, and a gentle pace

microcopy.line: One central area, without jumps between districts.

content.intro:
This day stays around the old Joseon capital: palace axis, museum by the gates, residential hanok area, temple pause, and Cheonggyecheon stream. The route does not try to show all of Seoul at once, but it gives a clear first map of the city.

You can walk from Gwanghwamun to Cheonggyecheon. In Bukchon, do not linger at house gates; if the upper streets are packed with groups, drop toward Anguk and add a cafe on the way.
content.routeLogic:
Start with Gyeongbokgung while group traffic is lighter.
After the palace, move into the museum so the whole day is not outdoors.
Pass through Bukchon quietly and without long photo sessions.
At Jogyesa, slow down before noisy Jongno.
At Cheonggyecheon, finish by the nearest metro exit or keep walking along the water if you still have energy.
content.bestFor:
a first visit
old center orientation
palaces and hanok
one day without distant transfers
content.notFor:
a late start
strong heat or heavy rain
travelers who want only modern Seoul
content.beforeYouGo:
Check palace and museum hours and closed days.
Follow quiet-tourism rules in Bukchon.
Part of the day is outdoors, so avoid heavy rain.
A transit card is useful for metro and buses.
content.timing: Start in the morning at Gwanghwamun: the palace and Bukchon are calmer before midday.
content.fallbackPlan: If the palace is closed or weather is poor, keep the museum and Jogyesa, and shorten Bukchon to a brief passage from Anguk.

stopList.name: Gyeongbokgung
stopList.note: The palace sets the old-center axis

content.context: Gyeongbokgung was the main Joseon palace and stands at the north edge of Gwanghwamun. In this route it sets the scale of the old capital: gates, courts, axial layout, and an easy move to the museum in the palace cluster.
content.lookFor:
How gates line up the movement
Open courts between pavilions
Mountain view behind the palace
Quieter side passages if the main court is crowded
content.practical:
Hours, closed days, and tickets change; check before leaving.
Most of the walk is outdoors.
The gate ceremony can draw a dense crowd.
content.nextMove: After the palace, move to the Folk Museum near the palace complex without planning a separate transfer.
content.sourceNote: Palace hours, closed days, and ticket rules can change; check current visitor information before leaving.

stopList.name: National Folk Museum
stopList.note: An indoor pause after the palace

content.context: The National Folk Museum of Korea shifts the day from palace architecture to everyday culture. It works well second: after large courts, you get an indoor pause while the route stays tied to the old center.
content.lookFor:
Seasonal and everyday stories in the permanent display
Objects that explain family and city rituals
Courtyard and exterior details between halls
content.practical:
Entry rules and temporary closures should be checked separately.
The children museum may require online booking.
This is a useful stop for heat, cold, or rain.
content.nextMove: Continue to Bukchon through the Anguk area; the walk is short, but hills can slow the pace.
content.sourceNote: Museum access rules and special areas can change; check current visitor information.

stopList.name: Bukchon
stopList.note: Residential hanok area: keep it quiet

content.context: Bukchon is not an open-air museum but a residential hanok area between palaces. In this route it is not about waiting for the same photo. It shows how the old city scale still lives beside modern streets.
content.lookFor:
Tile roof lines on narrow streets
Height changes between lanes
Closed gates as signs of private life
Views toward palace districts
content.practical:
Keep quiet and do not film inside homes.
Some zones may have visiting restrictions.
Small groups work better here.
content.nextMove: From Bukchon, go down toward Insadong and Jogyesa, choosing calmer lanes if the main streets are crowded.
content.sourceNote: Residential access and quiet-tourism rules can change; check current guidance before going.

stopList.name: Jogyesa
stopList.note: A calm temple stop

content.context: Jogyesa stands beside office and tourist Jongno, but the courtyard immediately slows the pace. After Bukchon it is a short, focused pause: the temple shows a Buddhist layer of the city without a long detour.
content.lookFor:
Main hall and painted eaves
Lanterns and seasonal decoration in the courtyard
How the temple court fits inside the dense center
content.practical:
Speak quietly and do not disturb services.
Some indoor areas may discourage photography.
If a ceremony is underway, stay with the courtyard.
content.nextMove: Walk from the temple to Cheonggyecheon: it is a short move from a quiet courtyard to open city water.

stopList.name: Cheonggyecheon
stopList.note: Finish with a walk along the water

content.context: Cheonggyecheon brings the route back from palaces and lanes into the modern center. It works as a finish because you can walk only as far as you have energy, exit to metro at several points, or sit by the water.
content.lookFor:
Steps down from street level
Bridges and stone crossings
The shift from street noise to the lower level
Evening light if you finish near dusk
content.practical:
After heavy rain, access to the lower level can change.
Festivals and weekends can make it busier.
Keep a metro exit as plan B.
content.nextMove: End at the nearest metro station or continue along the stream toward Jongno if you still have energy.

title: Hanok And Insadong
subtitle: Seochon / Bukchon
blurb: Residential streets, hanok, and Insadong in a slow walking loop.

microcopy.line: Less checklist, more streets and pauses.

content.intro:
This half-day is for old neighborhoods without the scale of a palace. Seochon gives a more everyday start west of Gyeongbokgung, Bukchon keeps the hanok line, Insadong adds shops and galleries, and Jogyesa closes the loop with a quiet stop.

The route is deliberately short. Here it is better to move slowly and notice the shifts between residential streets and commercial quarters rather than collect the maximum number of sights.
content.routeLogic:
Seochon sets a local tone before the tourist core.
Treat Bukchon as a residential area, not a photo set.
Insadong is useful for coffee, galleries, and craft windows.
Jogyesa finishes the route near the main Jongno streets.
content.bestFor:
a slow walk
hanok and lanes
a half-day without museums
a repeat visit
content.notFor:
large groups
poor weather
travelers who want to enter palaces
content.beforeYouGo:
Do not plan loud group photo sessions in Bukchon.
Some shops and galleries keep their own hours.
In rain, trade some street time for cafes or Insadong galleries.
content.timing: Go in the morning or on a quieter weekday: narrow streets read better without heavy crowds.
content.fallbackPlan: If Bukchon is overloaded or restricted, shorten it to a quiet passage and give Insadong more time.

stopList.name: Seochon
stopList.note: Residential streets introduce the old center

content.context: Seochon lies west of Gyeongbokgung and feels calmer than the main palace approaches. It is a soft start to the route: small streets, low buildings, cafes, and workshops set a neighborhood scale rather than a ceremonial one.
content.lookFor:
Low facades beside newer cafes
Narrow side streets without dense flow
Mountain glimpses between houses
Old gates and small workshops
content.practical:
This is a living district, not one museum object.
Do not enter courtyards without an invitation.
Check cafes and workshops on the spot.
content.nextMove: Walk toward Bukchon through the north side of the palace area, leaving time for hills.

stopList.name: Bukchon
stopList.note: The hanok are homes; keep a quiet pace

content.context: On this route, Bukchon links two old-neighborhood worlds: more local Seochon and the shop-and-gallery side of Insadong. Remember that behind the beautiful roofs are ordinary homes and everyday life.
content.lookFor:
Roof tiles and wooden hanok details
How streets narrow on the climb
Behavior-rule signs
Places where it is better to keep walking than stop
content.practical:
Keep your voice lower than usual.
Do not photograph interiors or open doors.
Some zones may have time restrictions.
content.nextMove: Go down toward Insadong through the Anguk side, where the route naturally shifts from homes to shops and galleries.
content.sourceNote: Residential access and quiet-tourism rules can change; check current guidance before going.

stopList.name: Insadong
stopList.note: Windows and galleries after Bukchon

content.context: Insadong gathers craft shops, tea rooms, galleries, and souvenir windows near the old center. After Bukchon, it is the more public layer of the neighborhood: a place to pause, buy small things, and stay within the walking logic.
content.lookFor:
Side galleries, not only the main street
Signs and windows with traditional materials
Tea rooms upstairs or in courtyards
content.practical:
Shop and gallery hours differ.
Weekend flow is noticeably denser.
Keep purchases light; the temple is still ahead.
content.nextMove: The walk from Insadong to Jogyesa is short; use quieter side streets if the main axis is noisy.

stopList.name: Jogyesa
stopList.note: A calm finish near Insadong

content.context: Jogyesa closes the neighborhood route without a long transfer. After Insadong windows, the temple courtyard brings attention back to quiet, roof details, and seasonal decoration, then it is easy to return to metro or Jongno cafes.
content.lookFor:
Main hall among city buildings
Colorful lanterns if they are up
Painted details under the eaves
People who came for worship, not tourism
content.practical:
Do not block passages or disturb prayer.
During ceremonies, stay at the edge of the courtyard.
Treat the temple as a short stop, not a photo set.
content.nextMove: Finish at Jonggak or Anguk station, or return to Insadong for food and tea.

title: Market And DDP
subtitle: Jongno / Dongdaemun
blurb: The stream, Gwangjang Market, and evening Dongdaemun without extra detours.

microcopy.line: Leave time for the market and pauses by the stream.

content.intro:
The evening route follows a simple line: first the water of Cheonggyecheon, then the food and noise of Gwangjang, then the walk to Dongdaemun Gate and DDP. It is not a dish checklist; it is a clear evening rhythm in the center.

Crowds can tire you quickly here, so do not plan a full dinner at every point. Make one main market pause, leave space for walking, and keep a backup metro exit.
content.routeLogic:
The stream gives a calmer entry into evening.
Gwangjang becomes the main food stop.
Dongdaemun links the market to the design district.
DDP works as the finish with architecture and a possible indoor pause.
content.bestFor:
an evening in the center
market and street food
short walks
a route after daytime plans
content.notFor:
crowd intolerance
strict diets
a late start without checking transport
content.beforeYouGo:
Do not promise yourself exact dishes: stalls and queues change.
Carry cash or a card with backup payment options.
Check DDP events if you expect to go inside.
content.timing: Start one or two hours before dinner: the market becomes the pause, and DDP stays for evening light.
content.fallbackPlan: If the market is too dense, pass through briefly and move dinner to the DDP or Jongno area.

stopList.name: Cheonggyecheon
stopList.note: The stream eases you into evening

content.context: Cheonggyecheon is useful before the market: it runs below street level and softens the noise of the center. In the evening, the stream lets you start without jumping straight into the crowd, then leads naturally toward Jongno and Gwangjang.
content.lookFor:
Bridges and steps down to the water
Light on the lower promenade
Places where it is easy to exit to street level
The change from office area to market streets
content.practical:
After rain, access to the water can change.
Events and holidays make the flow denser.
Keep the route flexible: you can exit earlier.
content.nextMove: Walk from the stream to Gwangjang Market; it is a short transfer, after which the pace becomes much tighter.

stopList.name: Gwangjang
stopList.note: The market is the main food stop

content.context: Gwangjang is one of Seoul's best-known traditional markets, but in this route it is not a checkbox. It is the main food block of the evening: noisy rows, fast seating, simple navigation, and the chance to try a few small portions.
content.lookFor:
Rows with pancakes, gimbap, and snacks
Seating right at counters
Fabric and clothing sections outside the food rows
Queues that move quickly
content.practical:
Prices, hours, and dishes depend on the specific stalls.
At peak times it is tight, so keep belongings close.
Choose one or two stops instead of the whole market at once.
content.nextMove: After the market, move toward Dongdaemun: a short walk helps shift from food noise to open city evening.
content.sourceNote: Market hours, stalls, and dish availability vary; check the current situation on site.

stopList.name: Dongdaemun
stopList.note: The district adds city evening after the market

content.context: Dongdaemun links the market with DDP and reminds you the area is not only shopping. The historic gate and broad transport hub give a short outdoor block between food and the architectural finish.
content.lookFor:
Heunginjimun Gate against moving traffic
The shift between old object and modern shopping district
Wide crossings where direction matters
content.practical:
The district is lively; cross streets only at clear crossings.
Shops and malls keep different hours.
If you are tired, go straight to DDP or the metro.
content.nextMove: DDP is nearby; the walk is short, but leave a few minutes for lights and orientation in the large interchange.

stopList.name: DDP
stopList.note: The final point by Dongdaemun

content.context: Dongdaemun Design Plaza finishes the evening with modern scale: flowing building forms, plazas, and exhibition spaces feel sharply different from the market. Even without an exhibition, it is a useful final point by the metro.
content.lookFor:
Curving facade and level changes
Open plazas around the building
Current exhibition and event posters
Night light on metal surfaces
content.practical:
Exhibitions, entry, and events change, so check before visiting.
Part of the route may be outdoors; account for weather.
Late in the evening, keep the nearest station as your exit point.
content.nextMove: End at Dongdaemun History and Culture Park station, or stay in the area only if you already checked return transport.
content.sourceNote: DDP exhibitions, paid areas, and events change; check the current program before going inside.

title: Museum Day
subtitle: Yongsan / Itaewon
blurb: Yongsan museums and a short Itaewon finish for bad weather.

microcopy.line: Works when a long outdoor walk is inconvenient.

content.intro:
This day is built for heat, rain, dust, or simple fatigue from street routes. The National Museum of Korea gives a large culture block, the War Memorial adds modern history, Leeum shifts the day toward art, and Itaewon leaves a short neighborhood finish.

Treat transfers between points as part of the plan, not a route mistake. This is not a continuous walk; it is a calm day with long halls and short outdoor moves.
content.routeLogic:
Start with the National Museum while you have energy for a large site.
Ride rather than walk the whole way to the War Memorial.
Leeum adds a compact art block.
Itaewon is kept as a short finish near Yongsan.
content.bestFor:
rain or heat
museums
a slow day
a break from long streets
content.notFor:
travelers who dislike museums
a full-day walk on foot
children with no interest in halls
content.beforeYouGo:
Check closed days and special exhibitions.
Museums can have rules for food, photos, and loud conversation.
Metro or taxi is easier between sites.
content.timing: Start in the morning at the National Museum: the first stop can take more time than it looks.
content.fallbackPlan: If energy is lower, keep only the National Museum and Leeum, and use Itaewon as a short nearby dinner.

stopList.name: National Museum of Korea
stopList.note: The main culture stop of the day

content.context: The National Museum of Korea is the main large block of this calm day. It lets you spend several hours indoors without losing the route: the collections give broad context for history and art, and Yongsan works well for the next stops.
content.lookFor:
Permanent halls instead of racing through every floor
Stone objects and the garden if weather allows
Quiet passages between major sections
Museum shop as a short pause
content.practical:
The permanent collection is often free, while special exhibitions may be paid.
Check closed days and hours before visiting.
Keep quiet in the halls and follow photo rules.
content.nextMove: Ride to the War Memorial by metro, bus, or taxi; walking the full way would make the day too heavy.
content.sourceNote: Museum hours, closed days, and special exhibitions can change; check current visitor information.

stopList.name: War Memorial
stopList.note: Continues the Yongsan museum theme

content.context: The War Memorial of Korea adds a complex historical layer to the museum day. It fits logistically with the National Museum in Yongsan, but it is emotionally heavier: move selectively, without trying to cover every hall.
content.lookFor:
Outdoor equipment and memorial spaces
Halls connected to the Korean War
Places to pause between heavy sections
content.practical:
Check hours and access to individual areas before going.
The subject can be heavy for children and sensitive visitors.
Leave time to rest after the visit.
content.nextMove: For Leeum, ride toward Hannam-dong and Hangangjin; do not plan a long walking link.

stopList.name: Leeum
stopList.note: An art block after history

content.context: Leeum Museum of Art moves the day from history toward art and architecture. After large state museums it feels more compact, but it still needs attention to booking and current exhibitions.
content.lookFor:
Differences between M1 and M2 spaces
Traditional materials beside contemporary art
Stairs, light, and interior transitions
The current exhibition if it fits your timing
content.practical:
The museum runs by schedule and often recommends booking.
Some exhibitions are paid, others may be free.
Avoid bringing large bags or food into the galleries.
content.nextMove: After Leeum, walk down toward Itaewon or ride briefly if weather or energy makes the street unappealing.
content.sourceNote: Leeum booking, tickets, and exhibition access can change; check current visit information.

stopList.name: Itaewon
stopList.note: A final walk near Yongsan

content.context: Itaewon ends the museum day not with a monument but with a change of urban scene. After halls and memorial spaces, the district gives a short walk, food, and a clear metro exit without another major site.
content.lookFor:
Signs and food from many countries
Street level changes between Hannam and Itaewon
Calmer side streets instead of the main crowd
content.practical:
In the evening, the district can be noisy and dense.
Choose food by the current situation, not old lists.
If you are tired, finish at the metro right away.
content.nextMove: End at Itaewon or Hangangjin station, whichever is closer to dinner and your return route.

title: River And Tower
subtitle: Seoul Forest / Hangang
blurb: Hangang parks and Namsan tower as a clear-weather finish.

microcopy.line: Leave the tower for a clear final view.

content.intro:
This is the most open Seoul route in the seed: park, several river points, and an observation tower at the end. It does not pretend to be one continuous walk because the Hangang is wide, and the best viewpoints are better linked by metro or taxi.

The plan depends on weather more than the others. In heat, shorten daytime stretches by the water; in poor visibility, do not cling to the tower; in good weather, leave more time for the finish.
content.routeLogic:
Seoul Forest starts the day green and calm.
Yeouido gives a wide riverbank and simple metro access.
Banpo adds another angle on the Hangang.
N Seoul Tower works only as a weather-dependent finish.
content.bestFor:
clear weather
parks and river
a family pace
a full day without museums
content.notFor:
heavy rain or strong heat
poor visibility
travelers who want to walk all day without transport
content.beforeYouGo:
Check forecast, fine dust, and visibility.
For river parks, bring water and sun protection.
Fountain, event, and tower schedules change.
Plan metro or taxi between parks.
content.timing: Start before midday and leave the tower for evening or late day, when sky clarity is easier to judge.
content.fallbackPlan: If visibility is poor, replace the tower with dinner by the river or a short indoor plan near the closest station.

stopList.name: Seoul Forest
stopList.note: The park starts the day away from city noise

content.context: Seoul Forest gives a green start before the larger river spaces. It is useful for an easy opening: walk the alleys, take a child or coffee pause, and avoid entering the day through the dense center.
content.lookFor:
Broad paths and shaded stretches
Places where children can move more freely
Transitions toward Seongsu if you want coffee
Seasonal flowers or open lawns
content.practical:
The park is mostly outdoors, so weather matters.
Weekends can be denser near popular entrances.
Do not stretch the stop too long; a transfer to the Hangang is ahead.
content.nextMove: Ride the metro to Yeouido Hangang; the distance across the city is too long for a walking link.

stopList.name: Yeouido Hangang
stopList.note: The Hangang bank moves the route to the river

content.context: Yeouido Hangang Park shows the river wide and open, with easy metro access. In the route, this is the main daytime bank: walk, sit on the grass, and watch the city open on both sides of the water.
content.lookFor:
Wide Hangang view
Lawns and picnic areas
Bridges and bicycle movement
Seasonal events if they do not block the walk
content.practical:
In summer, sun and heat tire you quickly.
Events and festivals can change the flow of people.
Check rentals and services on site.
content.nextMove: Ride rather than walk all the way to Banpo; this keeps energy for the finish.
content.sourceNote: Hangang events, rentals, and access can change; check the current situation on site.

stopList.name: Banpo Hangang
stopList.note: A second river point keeps the day by the water

content.context: Banpo gives a different angle on the Hangang and keeps the river day from becoming a single embankment. If fountains or events are running, treat them as a bonus; the route should not depend on them because schedules are seasonal and weather-based.
content.lookFor:
View toward the bridge and opposite bank
Evening light on the water
Places where you can sit calmly
Signs of current events or closures
content.practical:
Fountain schedules and access depend on season and weather.
It can be windy by the water.
Keep time in reserve for the move to Namsan.
content.nextMove: For N Seoul Tower, use metro, bus, taxi, or cable car depending on the day; it is a separate final block.
content.sourceNote: Fountain schedules, river access, and events can change with season and weather.

stopList.name: N Seoul Tower
stopList.note: Final view if the weather is clear

content.context: Leave N Seoul Tower for the end, when visibility is easier to judge. After parks and river, the tower gathers the day from above: you can see how the Hangang, Namsan, and central districts connect.
content.lookFor:
Air clarity before buying a ticket
Views of the Hangang curves
Dusk if the day is clear
Return route down before transport closes
content.practical:
Check hours, tickets, and the way up in advance.
In poor visibility, the view may not be worth the effort.
Sunset can bring queues.
content.nextMove: End the route by descending to the nearest transport; do not plan another distant point after the tower.
content.sourceNote: Tower hours, tickets, visibility, and access can change; check before committing to the final stop.
